content:
# Exodus Chapter 4 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~33r–33v (PDF pp. 64–65); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 44–45 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_004_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_004_page_002.jpg`

---

**4:1** And Moses answered and said, "But behold, they will not believe me and will not listen to my voice, for they will say, 'YHWH did not appear to you.'"

**4:2** And YHWH said to him, "What is that in your hand?" And he said, "A staff."

**4:3** And He said, "Cast it on the ground." And he cast it on the ground, and it became a serpent; and Moses fled from before it.

**4:4** And YHWH said to Moses, "Stretch out your hand and seize it by its tail." And he stretched out his hand and took hold of it, and it became a staff in his palm.

**4:5** "So that they may believe that YHWH, the God of their fathers -- the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob -- appeared to you."

**4:6** And YHWH said to him further, "Now put your hand into your bosom." And he put his hand into his bosom, and he brought it out, and behold, his hand was leprous, like snow.

**4:7** And He said, "Put your hand back into your bosom." And he put his hand back into his bosom, and he brought it out from his bosom, and behold, it had returned to be like his flesh.

**4:8** "And it shall be, if they will not believe you and will not heed the voice of the first sign, then they will believe the voice of the latter sign.

**4:9** And it shall be, if they will not believe even these two signs and will not listen to your voice, then you shall take from the water of the Nile and pour it on the dry ground; and the water that you take from the Nile shall become blood on the dry ground."

**4:10** And Moses said to YHWH, "Please, my Lord, I am not a man of words, neither from yesterday nor from the day before nor from the time You have spoken to Your servant, for I am heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue." [fn1]

**4:11** And YHWH said to him, "Who placed a mouth for man? Or who makes him mute, or deaf, or seeing, or blind? Is it not I, YHWH?

**4:12** And now, go, and I Myself will be with your mouth, and I will teach you what you shall speak."

**4:13** And he said, "Please, my Lord, send, I pray, by the hand of whomever You will send."

**4:14** And the anger of YHWH burned against Moses, and He said, "Is there not Aaron your brother, the Levite? I know that he can surely speak. And also, behold, he is coming out to meet you, and he will see you and will rejoice in his heart.

**4:15** And you shall speak to him and put the words in his mouth; and I Myself will be with your mouth and with his mouth, and I will teach you what you shall do.

**4:16** And he shall speak for you to the people, and it shall be that he shall be for you as a mouth, and you shall be for him as God.

**4:17** And this staff you shall take in your hand, with which you shall do the signs."

**4:18** And Moses went and returned to Jethro his father-in-law and said to him, "Let me go, I pray, and return to my brothers who are in Egypt, that I may see whether they are still alive." And Jethro said to Moses, "Go in peace." [fn2]

**4:19** And YHWH said to Moses in Midian, "Go, return to Egypt, for all the men who were seeking your life have died."

**4:20** And Moses took his wife and his sons and set them upon the donkey, and he returned to the land of Egypt. And Moses took the staff of God in his hand.

**4:21** And YHWH said to Moses, "When you go to return to Egypt, see all the wonders that I have put in your hand, and you shall do them before Pharaoh. But I will strengthen his heart, and he will not send the people out. [fn3]

**4:22** And you shall say to Pharaoh, 'Thus says YHWH: My son, My firstborn, is Israel.

**4:23** And I say to you: Send out My son that he may serve Me. And if you refuse to send him out, behold, I am about to kill your son, your firstborn.'"

**4:24** And it was on the way, at the lodging place, that YHWH met him and sought to kill him. [fn4]

**4:25** And Zipporah took a flint and cut off the foreskin of her son, and she touched it to his feet. And she said, "For you are a bridegroom of blood to me." [fn4]

**4:26** And He withdrew from him. Then she said, "A bridegroom of blood" -- on account of the circumcision. [fn4]

**4:27** And YHWH said to Aaron, "Go to the wilderness to meet Moses." And he went and met him at the mountain of God and kissed him.

**4:28** And Moses told Aaron all the words of YHWH with which He had sent him, and all the signs with which He had commanded him.

**4:29** And Moses and Aaron went and gathered all the elders of the sons of Israel.

**4:30** And Aaron spoke all the words that YHWH had spoken to Moses, and he did the signs before the eyes of the people.

**4:31** And the people believed. And they heard that YHWH had attended to the sons of Israel and that He had seen their affliction. And they bowed down and worshiped.

---

> **[fn1] v.10** — Hebrew: *kevad peh ukhvad lashon* (כְבַד־פֶּה וּכְבַד לָשׁוֹן), literally "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue." The adjective *kaved* ("heavy, weighty") is from the root k-v-d, the same root that produces *kavod* ("glory") and that will later describe Pharaoh's "heavy" heart (7:14, 8:15, 9:7). Whether Moses describes a stutter, a speech impediment, slowness of speech, or lack of eloquence, the Hebrew does not specify. The LXX interprets as *ischophonos kai braduglossos* ("weak-voiced and slow-tongued"). The SOPHIA consensus preserves the literal Hebrew image, following EMET and LOGOS, because the wordplay with the hardening of Pharaoh's heart is theologically significant: the same "heaviness" that afflicts the deliverer's mouth will afflict the oppressor's heart.

> **[fn2] v.18** — The Leningrad Codex reads *Yeter* (יֶתֶר) for the first occurrence of Moses' father-in-law's name in this verse, then *Yitro* (יִתְרוֹ) for the second. Both are forms of the same name (from the root y-t-r, "to abound"). The LXX harmonizes both to *Iothor*. EMET preserves both forms; LOGOS and PNEUMA use "Jethro" throughout. The SOPHIA consensus follows the majority in rendering "Jethro" for consistency with the established rendering in 3:1, while noting the textual variation.

> **[fn3] v.21** — Hebrew: *va'ani achazzeq et-libbo* (וַאֲנִי אֲחַזֵּק אֶת־לִבּוֹ). The verb is the Piel of *chazaq* (ח-ז-ק), meaning "to make strong, to firm up, to fortify." This is the first of three distinct Hebrew verbs used for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart throughout Exodus: *chazaq* ("to strengthen/firm"), *kaved* ("to make heavy"), and *qashah* ("to make stiff/hard"). The SOPHIA consensus renders "strengthen" rather than "harden" at this first occurrence because (1) *chazaq* in its primary sense means "to make strong" -- the same verb is used positively throughout the Hebrew Bible (e.g., "be strong and courageous," Joshua 1:6); (2) the LXX's *skleruno* ("to make hard/stubborn") introduces a negative connotation not present in the Hebrew root; and (3) preserving the distinction between the three hardening verbs allows the reader to track the theological development across the plague narrative. See Council Note 4 below.

> **[fn4] vv.24-26** — The *chatan damim* ("bridegroom of blood") passage. Hebrew: *chatan damim* (חֲתַן דָּמִים). This is among the most enigmatic passages in all of Scripture. The ambiguities are multiple and the SOPHIA consensus preserves them without resolution: (a) Whom does YHWH seek to kill? The pronoun "him" most naturally refers to Moses, the last named masculine subject, but some interpreters argue it refers to the uncircumcised son. (b) Whose "feet" (*raglav*, רַגְלָיו) does Zipporah touch? The word *regel* can be a euphemism for genitals (cf. Isaiah 7:20, Ruth 3:7). (c) What does *chatan damim* mean? The word *chatan* can mean "bridegroom," "son-in-law," or "one connected by kinship." In cognate Arabic, the root kh-t-n means "to circumcise," suggesting a deep etymological link between circumcision and kinship. (d) Why "circumcisions" (*mulot*, מוּלֹת) in the plural? The word is found only here in the Hebrew Bible. The LXX substantially reinterprets: it reads "angel of the Lord" for YHWH (theological softening) and "the blood of the circumcision of my child has stopped" for Zipporah's declaration. The SOPHIA consensus follows the MT, preserving the strangeness. See Council Note 5 and the Disagreement Log below.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### 1. "Staff" vs. "Rod" (vv. 2, 4, 17, 20)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "rod" throughout.
- **LOGOS:** "staff" throughout.
- **PNEUMA:** "staff" throughout.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Staff" is adopted, following the 2-to-1 majority (LOGOS and PNEUMA). Both "rod" and "staff" translate Hebrew *matteh* adequately. "Staff" better evokes the shepherd's instrument that Moses carries, which is contextually fitting as he has been tending Jethro's flock.

### 2. "Serpent" vs. "Snake" (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "nakhash [serpent]" -- transliteration with gloss.
- **LOGOS:** "serpent."
- **PNEUMA:** "snake."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Serpent" is adopted, following EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew *nachash* is the same word used in Genesis 3:1, and "serpent" preserves that intertextual resonance in English. "Snake" is accurate but lacks the literary and theological weight that the English tradition has attached to "serpent."

### 3. "Fled" vs. "Recoiled" (v. 3)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "fled from before it."
- **LOGOS:** "fled from before it."
- **PNEUMA:** "recoiled from it."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Fled from before it" is adopted, following EMET and LOGOS. The Hebrew *vayyanas* (from *nus*) means "to flee, to run away" -- not a flinch but an act of running. PNEUMA's "recoiled" is evocative but narrows the physical response. The verb *nus* appears throughout the military and flight narratives of the Hebrew Bible with the unambiguous sense of full retreat.

### 4. "Strengthen" vs. "Harden" -- v. 21 -- ⚠️ FLAGGED

**The three translators diverged:**
- **EMET:** "I will strengthen his heart" -- preserving the primary sense of *chazaq*.
- **LOGOS:** "I will harden his heart" -- following the traditional English rendering.
- **PNEUMA:** "I will harden his heart" -- matching LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Strengthen" is adopted, following EMET against the 2-to-1 majority. This is a case where the minority rendering is more faithful to the Hebrew. The reasons:

1. **The Hebrew verb *chazaq* means "to be strong."** Its Piel form (*chizzeq*) means "to make strong, to fortify." It is the verb in "be strong and courageous" (*chazaq ve'ematz*, Deuteronomy 31:6, Joshua 1:6). Rendering it as "harden" imports a connotation of stubbornness that belongs to *qashah*, not *chazaq*.
2. **Three verbs, three meanings.** Exodus uses *chazaq* ("strengthen"), *kaved* ("make heavy"), and *qashah* ("make hard/stiff") in a deliberate and distinct pattern. Collapsing *chazaq* and *qashah* both into "harden" destroys a distinction the text carefully maintains.
3. **The LXX introduced the flattening.** The LXX renders *chazaq* as *skleruno* ("to make hard/stubborn"), which carries a more negative valence than the Hebrew. The traditional English "harden" follows the LXX's interpretive choice, not the MT's semantic range.
4. **Theological precision.** "I will strengthen his heart" preserves the paradox more sharply than "I will harden his heart." God makes Pharaoh's resolve *strong* -- firm in the course Pharaoh has already chosen. This is theologically distinct from making him *hard* or *stubborn*, which implies an imposition foreign to his will.

This decision is flagged because two translators chose "harden" and the traditional weight of English Bibles supports it. The SOPHIA consensus accepts the cost of departing from convention in order to honor the Hebrew distinction.

### 5. The *Chatan Damim* Passage (vv. 24-26) -- ⚠️ FLAGGED

**The three translators diverged in nuance:**
- **EMET:** Maximally literal: "she caused-[it]-to-touch his feet"; "hatan damim [bridegroom of blood]"; "he withdrew from him"; "hatan damim -- for the mulot [circumcisions]." Preserves Hebrew terms in transliteration. Leaves all pronominal ambiguities unresolved.
- **LOGOS:** Formal: "touched it to his feet"; "a bridegroom of blood to me"; "He relented from him"; "A bridegroom of blood, on account of the circumcision." Resolves "he" as YHWH via capitalization ("He relented"). Uses "relented" for *vayyiref*.
- **PNEUMA:** Dynamic: "touched it to his feet"; "You are a bridegroom of blood to me"; "he let him alone"; "A bridegroom of blood -- she had said it because of the circumcision." Adds "Moses" as the object of divine assault. Uses "let him alone" for *vayyiref*.

**SOPHIA's decision:** The consensus preserves the passage's irreducible strangeness while making minimal clarifying choices:

1. **"YHWH met him and sought to kill him"** -- The subject is YHWH (all three agree on this from the MT; the LXX's "angel of the Lord" is a theological softening). The object "him" is left unresolved, as in the Hebrew. PNEUMA's insertion of "Moses" is interpretive but not mandated by the text.
2. **"touched it to his feet"** -- All three agree on this rendering. The ambiguity of "his" and the possible euphemistic sense of "feet" are noted in the footnote.
3. **"bridegroom of blood"** -- All three translators render *chatan damim* as "bridegroom of blood." The consensus retains this. The word *chatan* is not transliterated (as EMET partially did) because "bridegroom" captures the primary English sense and the strangeness of the phrase is already sufficient to mark it as extraordinary.
4. **"He withdrew from him"** -- EMET's "he withdrew" and LOGOS's "He relented" both render *vayyiref* (from *raphah*, "to let go, release, sink"). PNEUMA's "let him alone" is too domesticated. The consensus adopts "He withdrew" -- capitalized to indicate YHWH as subject (following LOGOS's convention), using EMET's verb choice because *raphah* denotes physical withdrawal/release more than cognitive relenting.
5. **"on account of the circumcision"** -- LOGOS's rendering is adopted over EMET's transliterated *mulot*. The plural *mulot* is noted in the footnote.

This passage is flagged because no consensus can resolve what the text itself leaves unresolved. The three translators agreed more than they disagreed here -- all preserved the essential strangeness -- but the divergences in verb choice and pronoun resolution reflect genuine interpretive uncertainty that readers must be trusted to hold.

### 6. "My son, My firstborn, is Israel" vs. "Israel is My son, My firstborn" (v. 22)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "My son, my firstborn, [is] Yisra'el" -- follows MT word order (*beni bekhori Yisra'el*).
- **LOGOS:** "Israel is My son, My firstborn" -- inverts to standard English subject-predicate order.
- **PNEUMA:** "Israel is my son, my firstborn" -- matches LOGOS.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "My son, My firstborn, is Israel" is adopted, following the MT word order as EMET rendered it. The Hebrew places *beni bekhori* ("My son, My firstborn") first, before the name *Yisra'el*. This order creates a rhetorical effect: the possessive claim comes before the identification. YHWH first asserts the relationship -- *My son, My firstborn* -- and only then names who it is. The word order is the theology: relationship precedes identity.

### 7. "Leprous" vs. "Diseased" (v. 6)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "leprous as snow."
- **LOGOS:** "leprous, like snow."
- **PNEUMA:** "diseased, white as snow."

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Leprous, like snow" is adopted, following EMET and LOGOS. While PNEUMA correctly notes that Hebrew *tsara'at* encompasses a broader range of skin conditions than Hansen's disease, "leprous" is the traditional formal equivalent that maintains the severity and ritual-impurity connotation of the Hebrew term. The word "diseased" is too general and loses the specific association with *tsara'at* that recurs in Leviticus 13-14.

### 8. "Attended to" (v. 31)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "visited" -- traditional rendering of *paqad*.
- **LOGOS:** "attended to" -- preserving the caring, supervisory sense.
- **PNEUMA:** "turned his attention to" -- dynamic rendering.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Attended to" is adopted, following LOGOS and the established SOPHIA rendering of *paqad* from 3:16. The verbal echo between 3:16 (*paqod paqadti*, "I have surely attended to") and 4:31 (*paqad YHWH*, "YHWH attended to") is theologically essential: God promised to attend, and now the people hear that He has. Consistency in rendering preserves this narrative closure.

### 9. "Instruct" vs. "Teach" (vv. 12, 15)

**The three translators:**
- **EMET:** "instruct" -- formal rendering of *yarah* (Hiphil).
- **LOGOS:** "instruct" -- matching EMET.
- **PNEUMA:** "teach" -- dynamic equivalent.

**SOPHIA's decision:** "Teach" is adopted, following PNEUMA. The Hebrew *yarah* (Hiphil: *horah*) means "to direct, instruct, teach" -- it is the root from which *Torah* derives. While "instruct" is acceptable, "teach" is more natural English and preserves the connection to *Torah* more transparently for the English reader. Both EMET and LOGOS acknowledged this semantic range. The shift from "instruct" does not sacrifice accuracy.

### 10. "Voice of the sign" (v. 8)

All three translators preserved the Hebrew idiom *qol ha'ot* -- signs have a "voice." EMET and LOGOS retained "voice of the sign"; PNEUMA paraphrased as "respond to the first sign." The consensus follows EMET and LOGOS. The personification of signs as having a communicative "voice" is a striking Hebrew idiom worth preserving.

### 11. "Sons of Israel" vs. "Israelites" (vv. 29, 31)

Following the established SOPHIA convention from Exodus 3: "sons of Israel" (*bney Yisra'el*) is retained throughout, following EMET and LOGOS. PNEUMA used "Israelites" in v. 29. The familial-covenantal connotation is preserved.

---

## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### ⚠️ v.21 — "Strengthen" vs. "Harden" (*chazaq* Piel)
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will strengthen his heart" | Literal: primary sense of *chazaq* |
| LOGOS | "I will harden his heart" | Formal: traditional English rendering, following LXX *skleruno* |
| PNEUMA | "I will harden his heart" | Dynamic: matching traditional convention |

**Nature of disagreement:** Whether to render the Piel of *chazaq* as "strengthen" (preserving the Hebrew semantic range and the distinction among three hardening verbs) or "harden" (following the LXX and the near-unanimous English tradition from KJV through ESV/NIV/NRSV).

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Strengthen" is adopted, following EMET against the majority. The SOPHIA Bible privileges the MT over the LXX, and the Hebrew *chazaq* is semantically distinct from *qashah* ("to make hard"). Collapsing both into "harden" obscures a distinction the text maintains. See Council Note 4 above.

**Severity:** HIGH. This decision affects the entire plague narrative (Exodus 7-14), where the three hardening verbs must be rendered consistently. The reader's understanding of divine sovereignty and human agency in the hardening of Pharaoh's heart depends on whether the Hebrew distinctions are preserved or collapsed. The SOPHIA convention going forward: *chazaq* = "strengthen," *kaved* = "make heavy," *qashah* = "make hard."

### ⚠️ vv.24-26 — The *Chatan Damim* ("Bridegroom of Blood") Passage
| Translator | v.24 "sought to kill" | v.25 "touched his feet" | v.25 *chatan damim* | v.26 verb | v.26 gloss |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| EMET | "YHWH met him and sought to kill him" | "caused-[it]-to-touch his feet" | "hatan damim [bridegroom of blood]" | "he withdrew from him" | "hatan damim -- for the mulot [circumcisions]" |
| LOGOS | "the LORD met him and sought to kill him" | "touched it to his feet" | "a bridegroom of blood to me" | "He relented from him" | "A bridegroom of blood, on account of the circumcision" |
| PNEUMA | "YHWH met Moses and sought to kill him" | "touched it to his feet" | "You are a bridegroom of blood to me" | "he let him alone" | "A bridegroom of blood -- she had said it because of the circumcision" |

**Nature of disagreement:** Multiple interlocking ambiguities that no translator can fully resolve: (a) the identity of the one YHWH seeks to kill; (b) the referent of "his feet"; (c) the meaning of *chatan damim*; (d) the verb for divine withdrawal (*vayyiref*); (e) the significance of the plural *mulot*. The translators agreed on much (all rendered "bridegroom of blood," all preserved the essential narrative), but diverged on the verb in v.26 and on how much to clarify the pronouns.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** The consensus preserves the pronominal ambiguity of the MT, renders "He withdrew" (capitalizing the divine subject, using EMET's verb), and glosses *mulot* as "the circumcision" following LOGOS. The passage's strangeness is retained. See Council Note 5 and footnote [fn4] above.

**Severity:** HIGH. This is one of the most enigmatic passages in the Torah. No consensus can claim certainty where the text itself refuses it. The three translators collectively honored the passage's resistance to resolution, and the SOPHIA consensus follows their collective restraint.

### ⚠️ v.10 — Moses' Speech Impediment (*kevad peh ukhvad lashon*)
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" | Literal preservation of Hebrew image |
| LOGOS | "heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" | Formal: same as EMET, preserving k-v-d wordplay |
| PNEUMA | "My mouth is slow and my tongue is clumsy" | Dynamic: experiential rendering |

**Nature of disagreement:** Whether to preserve the literal Hebrew metaphor ("heavy") or render the experienced meaning ("slow," "clumsy"). All three agree on the underlying reality; the disagreement is about the English surface.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Heavy of mouth and heavy of tongue" is adopted, following EMET and LOGOS. The wordplay between *kaved* (heavy) in Moses' self-description and *kaved* (heavy/hardened) in the Pharaoh-hardening motif is theologically significant and must be preserved. See footnote [fn1].

**Severity:** MODERATE. The literal rendering is unusual English but immediately comprehensible. PNEUMA's rendering is more natural but sacrifices a deliberate Hebrew wordplay.

### ⚠️ v.22 — Word Order: "My son, My firstborn, is Israel"
| Translator | Rendering | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "My son, my firstborn, [is] Yisra'el" | Follows MT word order |
| LOGOS | "Israel is My son, My firstborn" | Standard English subject-predicate |
| PNEUMA | "Israel is my son, my firstborn" | Standard English subject-predicate |

**Nature of disagreement:** Whether to preserve the MT word order (*beni bekhori Yisra'el* -- "My son, My firstborn, Israel") or invert to natural English.

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "My son, My firstborn, is Israel" -- following EMET's word order. The Hebrew places the possessive claim first, creating a rhetorical arc: relationship before identity. See Council Note 6.

**Severity:** LOW. Both word orders convey the same theological content. The SOPHIA choice is stylistic, prioritizing the rhetorical force of the Hebrew sequence.

---

*Consilience achieved across 31 verses. Four items flagged with disagreements: v.10 (Moses' speech impediment), v.21 (strengthen vs. harden), v.22 (word order), and vv.24-26 (bridegroom of blood). The supreme crux of this chapter -- the chatan damim passage -- resists resolution by design. The SOPHIA consensus stands where the text stands: in the dark, at the lodging place, with blood on the flint.*

-- SOPHIA
